To truly live our why, we need balance, clarity of why, discipline of how, and consistency of what. By now, you should be getting clearer on your why. This video is going to turn the focus onto your hows. Think of your hows as your guiding principles or values. They are the actions we take every day to bring our why to life. Many individuals or organizations communicate their values as nouns. They'll use words like authentic or integrity. But we can't do nouns. We can only do verbs. Instead of authentic, you might use something like say and do the things you actually believe. Instead of integrity, use do the right thing, even when no one is looking. Our hows will enable us to find and create the environments where we can work and be at our best. I'll give you an example. If I were approached by a company who told me, David, we believe what you believe. We believe business works best when leaders put their people first. Can we hire you? I'd consider it because we're both inspired by a common cause. If they then said, we'd like you to develop a leadership workshop specifically for our middle managers. They are our biggest challenge. That would throw up a red flag for me. One of my hows is look at the big picture, which means I need to understand the full context of what I'm working on if I'm going to be successful. I would need to understand the things that were causing the challenges in middle management in order to do my best work. I would make sure that I had access to senior leadership and others who might be able to help me understand the full context of the issue before I embarked on the project. The really cool thing is when we filter opportunities through our hows, we're more likely to see succeed, and we're more likely to help those around us succeed too. Okay, you've already done a lot of the work on your hows by listing your contributions as action freighters. The contributions that didn't make it into your why statement are ideal candidates for your hows. They are probably already in the right form, and when put into action, they will help you bring your why to life. Hows should be distinct. It's time to narrow them down and eliminate any redundancy. From the thousands of people we've guided through this process, we've found that there are always five or fewer hows. If you list more than five hows, it usually means one of two things. Either one of your hows is already expressed in different words in your why statement, or some of your hows can be combined. To help you with this, think back to earlier in the course when we showed you how to combine overlapping themes into one. Read that process for all your remaining contribution themes. If you're struggling, go back to the stories that are behind the themes. Rather than focusing on the dictionary definition of the words in the themes, focus on the feeling they evoke. When narrowed down, your hows will fully express the actions you take to live your why. So getting you started, in the next exercise, we want you to define at least three distinct hows. Remember, a how is expressed as a verb or a short action phrase. When you look back at your remaining themes, many of them are likely already in this form. Others may need a quick tweak to get them there. So for example, if a theme was connection, then we would write a how to be something like connect with people in meaningful ways. And if another was always learned from others, we might say learn something from everyone. Go for it. Take a few minutes to define at least three hows and type them in on the next page.
